content.text:
Context: npm publish prep — README, .npmignore, MIT license, package.json polish
Objective: Prepare @kntic/links for public npm publish. Write a complete README.md, polish package.json metadata, verify .npmignore is correct, and do a dry-run publish check. The README is the product's first impression — make it good.

Guidelines:
1. Read .kntic/MEMORY.MD for current project context and memory.
2. Reference the project ADRs in .kntic/adrs/ for architectural standards.
3. Execute the task in the manifest.
4. Update the manifest status to 'ready_for_merge' once the task is complete.
5. Adhere to security best practices (Least Privilege) for any infrastructure changes.
6. Update .kntic/MEMORY.MD if you learned something new or changed the architecture.

Use the kntic-task-status skill to set the task status. You MUST call .kntic/lib/skills/kntic-task-status/set-status.sh .kntic/manifests/LINKS-012.json ready_for_merge when done, or .kntic/lib/skills/kntic-task-status/set-status.sh .kntic/manifests/LINKS-012.json needs_review if blocked.

Please proceed with the task defined in: .kntic/manifests/LINKS-012.json

content.thinking: Let me start by reading the manifest and memory files to understand the task.

{
  "task_id": "LINKS-012",
  "title": "npm publish prep \u2014 README, .npmignore, MIT license, package.json polish",
  "status": "in_progress",
  "priority": "low",
  "description": "Prepare @kntic/links for public npm publish. Write a complete README.md, polish package.json metadata, verify .npmignore is correct, and do a dry-run publish check. The README is the product's first impression \u2014 make it good.",
  "requirements": {
    "features": [
      "Write README.md at repo root with: project tagline, install command (npm install -g @kntic/links), quick start (5 commands to go from zero to deployed), full command reference table, links.yaml schema reference, theme screenshots section (placeholder for now \u2014 add note: 'screenshots coming soon'), contributing section, MIT license badge",
      "README tone: developer-first, direct, no corporate fluff. Show the commands, not adjectives.",
      "README quick start should work end-to-end: links init \u2192 links add \u2192 links deploy --self \u2192 open dist/index.html",
      "Polish package.json: add 'description' field, 'keywords' array (linktree, link-page, cli, privacy, self-hosted, developer), 'homepage' (https://kntic.ai), 'repository' (GitLab URL), 'bugs' URL, 'engines' (node >= 18), ensure 'files' array matches what should be published (src/, LICENSE, README.md \u2014 exclude .kntic/, skills/, Dockerfile.agent, kntic.yml)",
      "Verify .npmignore or package.json 'files' field correctly excludes KNTIC orchestration files from the published package",
      "Run `npm pack --dry-run` and verify the file list looks correct \u2014 only src/, package.json, README.md, LICENSE should be in the tarball",
      "Add a `prepublishOnly` script in package.json that runs a basic sanity check (e.g., node -e 'import(\"./src/cli.js\")'  or a simple lint)"
    ],
    "outputs": [
      "README.md (complete)",
      "package.json (polished)",
      ".npmignore (verified)"
    ]

# MEMORY.MD — Persistent Working Memory

>
> **Last Updated:** 2026-03-16 (LINKS-011 completed)

---

## 1. Current Project State

**Project:** Links
**Repository:** *(update with your gitlab host/group/project)*

### Completed Tasks (Merged)
| Task ID | Title | Key Output |
|---------|-------|------------|
| LINKS-001 | Repo scaffold — package.json, CLI entry point, project structure | `package.json`, `src/cli.js`, 10 command stubs, `LICENSE`, `.npmignore`, `.gitignore`, `package-lock.json` |
| LINKS-002 | links.yaml config schema and parser | `src/config.js` — DEFAULT_CONFIG, validateUrl, validateConfig, findConfig, readConfig, writeConfig; atomic writes via tmp+rename; no external validation deps |
| LINKS-003 | `links init` — scaffold a new link page project | `src/commands/init.js` — creates links.yaml with commented template, --force flag, optional [directory] arg, uses os.userInfo() for default name |
| LINKS-004 | `links add / remove / list` — link management commands | `src/commands/add.js`, `src/commands/remove.js`, `src/commands/list.js` — add/remove/list links in links.yaml; URL validation, case-insensitive label matching, --update flag, --json output, scheduled link display |
| LINKS-005 | `links deploy --self` — static HTML generator | `src/generator.js` — generatePage(config, options), filterScheduled(), loadThemeCSS(), inlineImage(); `src/commands/deploy.js` — --self, --out, --open flags; `src/themes/minimal-dark.css` — default theme; fully self-contained HTML with inlined CSS, base64 avatar, schedule filtering, XSS-safe escaping |
| LINKS-006 | Theme system — CSS-based, pluggable | `src/themes/loader.js` — loadTheme(name), listThemes(); `src/themes/README.md` — documents 17 CSS custom property contract; `minimal-dark.css` updated to standard tokens; directory-traversal protection via path.basename |
| LINKS-007 | 5 launch themes | `minimal-dark.css` (default, muted violet accent), `minimal-light.css` (off-white, indigo accent), `terminal.css` (green-on-black, cursor blink, scanline, JetBrains Mono), `glass.css` (glassmorphism, backdrop-filter blur, purple/blue gradient), `developer.css` (IDE aesthetic, KNTIC orange, left border bar, `$ open` prefix) — all 17 tokens + full selector coverage |
| LINKS-010 | Link scheduling — scheduled_from and scheduled_until | `src/utils.js` — isLinkActive(link, now); `src/config.js` — validateDate(str); `src/generator.js` — uses isLinkActive, adds `<!-- built at ... -->` comment; `src/commands/list.js` — ⏰ schedule display with →; `src/commands/add.js` — --from/--until flags (already present from LINKS-004) |
| LINKS-011 | Utility commands — config, open, status | `src/commands/config-cmd.js` — opens links.yaml in $EDITOR/$VISUAL/nano/vi; `src/commands/open-cmd.js` — opens domain in browser (or --local for dist/index.html); `src/commands/status.js` — displays config summary with chalk formatting |

### Active Services
- **Dashboard** → `http://localhost:8000`
- **Orchestrator** → `kntic-engine` container

---

## 2. Architectural Constraints

Refer to `.kntic/adrs/` for all platform-level constraints:

| ADR | File | Title | Key Rule |
|-----|------|-------|----------|
| ADR-001 | ADR-001-Bootstrapping.md | Manifest-Driven Orchestration | FSM statuses: todo, in_progress, refactoring, needs_review, ready_for_merge, merged, backlog |
| ADR-002 | ADR-002-Security-Isolation.md | Security Isolation — Least Privilege | Mount only what the service needs; default `:ro`; dashboard sandboxed |
| ADR-003 | ADR-003-Task-Schema.md | Task Manifest Schema | Required fields: task_id, title, status; omission rule (no null/empty) |
| ADR-004 | ADR-004-GIA-State-File-Contract.md | GIA State File Contract | `.kntic/gia/state.json` — atomic writes; pass/fail only; dashboard read-only |
| ADR-005 | ADR-005-Technical-Debt-Task-Convention.md | Technical Debt Task Convention | TD- prefix; mandatory MEMORY.MD §3 update on resolution |
| ADR-006 | ADR-006-ADR-Guidelines.md | ADR Guidelines | Mandatory sections: Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences |
| ADR-007 | ADR-007-Proposal-Format-And-Handling.md | Proposal Format and Handling | Proposals in `.kntic/docs/`; feeds into ADR pipeline |

---

## 3. Known Technical Debt

| ID | Description | Severity | Origin |
|----|-------------|----------|--------|
| TD-1 | Orchestrator (`kntic-engine`) mounts `.:/app` full project root — should be narrowed to specific paths for least-privilege compliance. | Medium | ADR-002 §1 |

---

## 4. Active Protocol Notes

### For Task Agents
1. **Read this file first** at the start of every session.
2. **Update this file last** after completing a task.
3. **Manifest lifecycle:** `in_progress` → `ready_for_merge`. Orchestrator sets `merged`.
4. **Scope discipline:** Only read/write files listed in your manifest's `context_scope`.
5. **Security first:** Default to `:ro` for volume mounts. Escalate only with justification (ADR-002 §6).
6. **Actions log:** Append one entry to the manifest's `actions` array per session (ADR-003).
7. **New manifests:** Apply `chmod 664` + `chown root:node` immediately after creating (ADR-002 §4).

### Volume Mount Strategy (ADR-002)
- Dashboard mounts are granular and `:ro` by default.
- Only `.kntic/manifests/` and `.kntic/docs/` are writable by the dashboard.
- The orchestrator (`kntic-engine`) currently mounts `.:/app` full access — narrowing this is tracked as TD-1 in ADR-002.
- Write access escalation requires manifest justification + ADR-002 §2 table update.

---

## 5. [Questions for Human]

_No open questions._

---

## 6. [Additional Info from Human]

_No additional info yet._

# Links



## Getting started

To make it easy for you to get started with GitLab, here's a list of recommended next steps.

Already a pro? Just edit this README.md and make it your own. Want to make it easy? [Use the template at the bottom](#editing-this-readme)!

## Add your files

* [Create](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/repository/web_editor/#create-a-file) or [upload](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/repository/web_editor/#upload-a-file) files
* [Add files using the command line](https://docs.gitlab.com/topics/git/add_files/#add-files-to-a-git-repository) or push an existing Git repository with the following command:

```
cd existing_repo
git remote add origin https://gitlab.kommune7.wien/kommune7/apps/links.git
git branch -M main
git push -uf origin main
```

## Integrate with your tools

* [Set up project integrations](https://gitlab.kommune7.wien/kommune7/apps/links/-/settings/integrations)

## Collaborate with your team

* [Invite team members and collaborators](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/members/)
* [Create a new merge request](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/merge_requests/creating_merge_requests/)
* [Automatically close issues from merge requests](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/issues/managing_issues/#closing-issues-automatically)
* [Enable merge request approvals](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/merge_requests/approvals/)
* [Set auto-merge](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/merge_requests/auto_merge/)

## Test and Deploy

Use the built-in continuous integration in GitLab.

* [Get started with GitLab CI/CD](https://docs.gitlab.com/ci/quick_start/)
* [Analyze your code for known vulnerabilities with Static Application Security Testing (SAST)](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/application_security/sast/)
* [Deploy to Kubernetes, Amazon EC2, or Amazon ECS using Auto Deploy](https://docs.gitlab.com/topics/autodevops/requirements/)
* [Use pull-based deployments for improved Kubernetes management](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/clusters/agent/)
* [Set up protected environments](https://docs.gitlab.com/ci/environments/protected_environments/)

***

# Editing this README

When you're ready to make this README your own, just edit this file and use the handy template below (or feel free to structure it however you want - this is just a starting point!). Thanks to [makeareadme.com](https://www.makeareadme.com/) for this template.

## Suggestions for a good README

Every project is different, so consider which of these sections apply to yours. The sections used in the template are suggestions for most open source projects. Also keep in mind that while a README can be too long and detailed, too long is better than too short. If you think your README is too long, consider utilizing another form of documentation rather than cutting out information.

## Name
Choose a self-explaining name for your project.

## Description
Let people know what your project can do specifically. Provide context and add a link to any reference visitors might be unfamiliar with. A list of Features or a Background subsection can also be added here. If there are alternatives to your project, this is a good place to list differentiating factors.

## Badges
On some READMEs, you may see small images that convey metadata, such as whether or not all the tests are passing for the project. You can use Shields to add some to your README. Many services also have instructions for adding a badge.

## Visuals
Depending on what you are making, it can be a good idea to include screenshots or even a video (you'll frequently see GIFs rather than actual videos). Tools like ttygif can help, but check out Asciinema for a more sophisticated method.

## Installation
Within a particular ecosystem, there may be a common way of installing things, such as using Yarn, NuGet, or Homebrew. However, consider the possibility that whoever is reading your README is a novice and would like more guidance. Listing specific steps helps remove ambiguity and gets people to using your project as quickly as possible. If it only runs in a specific context like a particular programming language version or operating system or has dependencies that have to be installed manually, also add a Requirements subsection.

## Usage
Use examples liberally, and show the expected output if you can. It's helpful to have inline the smallest example of usage that you can demonstrate, while providing links to more sophisticated examples if they are too long to reasonably include in the README.

## Support
Tell people where they can go to for help. It can be any combination of an issue tracker, a chat room, an email address, etc.

## Roadmap
If you have ideas for releases in the future, it is a good idea to list them in the README.

## Contributing
State if you are open to contributions and what your requirements are for accepting them.

For people who want to make changes to your project, it's helpful to have some documentation on how to get started. Perhaps there is a script that they should run or some environment variables that they need to set. Make these steps explicit. These instructions could also be useful to your future self.

You can also document commands to lint the code or run tests. These steps help to ensure high code quality and reduce the likelihood that the changes inadvertently break something. Having instructions for running tests is especially helpful if it requires external setup, such as starting a Selenium server for testing in a browser.

## Authors and acknowledgment
Show your appreciation to those who have contributed to the project.

## License
For open source projects, say how it is licensed.

## Project status
If you have run out of energy or time for your project, put a note at the top of the README saying that development has slowed down or stopped completely. Someone may choose to fork your project or volunteer to step in as a maintainer or owner, allowing your project to keep going. You can also make an explicit request for maintainers.

# KNTIC Links — Theme System

Themes are **plain CSS files** that live in `src/themes/`. No build step, no
preprocessor. Each file defines a complete visual identity for a link page by
implementing a fixed set of **CSS custom properties** (design tokens) on `:root`
and using them throughout.

---

## Quick start — creating a new theme

1. Copy an existing theme (e.g. `minimal-dark.css`) to a new file:

   ```bash
   cp src/themes/minimal-dark.css src/themes/my-theme.css
   ```

2. Edit the `:root` block to change colours, fonts, radii, etc.

3. Set the theme in your project's `links.yaml`:

   ```yaml
   theme: my-theme
   ```

4. Run `links deploy --self` to see the result.

---

## Custom property contract

No accounts, no tracking, no third-party servers. You own the HTML. --- ## Quick Start Five commands from zero to a deployed page: ```bash # 1. Install npm install -g @kntic/links # 2. Scaffold a new project links init my-page && cd my-page # 3. Add some links links add "GitHub" "https://github.com/you" links add "Blog" "https://your-blog.dev" # 4. Build a self-contained HTML file links deploy --self # 5. Open it open dist/index.html ``` That's it. `dist/index.html` is a single file — inline CSS, base64 avatar, no external requests. Drop it on any static host. --- ## Install ```bash npm install -g @kntic/links ``` Requires Node.js 18 or later. --- ## Command Reference | Command | Description | |---------|-------------| | `links init [directory]` | Scaffold a new `links.yaml` project. `--force` to overwrite. | | `links add

Usage: links [options] [command]

CLI tool for managing and deploying link-in-bio pages

Options:
  -V, --version                output the version number
  -h, --help                   display help for command

Commands:
  init [options] [directory]   Initialise a new links project
  add [options] <label> <url>  Add a new link
  remove <label>               Remove a link
  list [options]               List all links
  deploy [options]             Build and deploy the link page
  theme                        Switch and list themes
  qr [options] [url]           Generate a QR code for the link page URL
  config                       Open links.yaml in your default editor
  open [options]               Open the deployed page in a browser
  status                       Show current project status
  help [command]               display help for command
